Hi Atif. Hope you are well. I think Akber explained i am rebuilding a j40 land cruiser.  For this i am looking for a full Vitz steering column assembly, vitz eps ecu/controller (ideally from the same vehicle), full set of EPS wiring pigtails/connectors
one of the new switches already fit in the size hole

Yep, that hole does look about right for a standard round accessory switch.

So I’d do this:

- *keep that hole* for one new switch
- use the *big rectangular opening* for either:
  - a proper radio/head unit, or
  - a custom blanking/switch panel
- ignore the random smaller holes unless you really need them

My recommendation:
- put the most-used switch in the *existing round hole*
- make a *small plate* for the rectangular opening and mount the rest of the switches there in a tidy row
- that’ll look much cleaner than drilling more holes into the dash

If you want, I can mark out a *best dash layout* from this photo, like:
- switch here
- radio here
- blanks here

Nice, this is enough to give you a proper steer.

My take:

- the *6x9s physically fit best* in that big lower opening area
- but they should *not* just bolt to the bare metal edge and hang open into the door
- best setup is a *mounting board / door card panel* over the inner skin, then mount the 6x9s to that
- your old *active sub* is separate, and honestly looks pretty tired, so I would treat it as optional rather than build around it
- those *round speakers* are probably better kept for dash/kick/rear use, not that door opening

For the dash:
- yes, one of those holes does look usable for a *new switch*
- the extra round holes and the lower rectangular cutout look like previous-owner add-ons
- if you want it tidy, I’d avoid reusing every old hole and instead make a *clean switch panel / blanking plate*

Best practical plan:
1. *Refit/finish the dash first*
   - gauge cluster
   - switches in a neat grouped panel
   - blank off unused holes

2. *Make door cards*
   - thin ply, ABS, or aluminium-backed panel
   - use the existing perimeter fixing holes
   - then cut the speaker holes where you actually want them

3. *Use the 6x9s in the door cards*
   - one per door if you’ve got the pair
   - add foam gasket behind speaker rim
   - keep window/regulator/latch clearance in mind

4. *Skip the old sub unless it still works well*
   - if it works, tuck it under a seat or rear area
   - if not, don’t let it dictate the whole install

Important caution:
- on old utility doors, water gets in, so if speakers go in the doors:
  - use a moisture barrier
  - don’t let the magnet hit glass/mechanism
  - protect wiring well

My recommendation:
- *clean dash*
- *custom door cards*
- *pair of 6x9s*
- *leave the old sub out for now*

That’ll give you something much neater and probably better sounding too.

If you want, next I can sketch a *simple layout* for:
- which dash holes to use for switches
- where exactly to place the 6x9 on that door panel
- what material to make the door card from.

Between those choices, go OME — specifically OME leaf springs + OME Nitrocharger shocks, not Bilstein shocks alone. On a J40, the lift comes from the springs, not from swapping to a Bilstein shock. Bilstein’s current J40-fit parts are sold as B8 5125 custom shocks, and those product pages list them as shocks with no spring seat included; Bilstein’s ride-height-adjustable 5100 that actually adds lift does it with an adjustable spring perch for front coil-spring applications, which is not the setup your leaf-sprung FJ40 uses.

So the simple answer is: OME is the right choice if your goal is maximum usable height that still works properly. ARB’s 40-series catalog shows the front setup at about 35 mm lift with CS001F or CS002F springs and 60097 shocks, and the rear at about 50 mm lift with CS001R or CS002R springs. The same catalog also notes that 1975+ FJ40s may need N76 rear shocks if the rear lower shock eye is below the leaf spring.

My recommendation for your 1978 FJ40: OME Nitrocharger setup. Use CS002F + 60097 up front only if you have added front weight; otherwise CS001F + 60097. In the rear, choose the spring by your constant load, then match the shock to the mount style on your truck. That is the combo most likely to give you the most lift and still ride/work correctly, because OME tunes the shocks around the spring choice, accessory fitment, and load.

shackle reversal on a Land Cruiser J40/FJ40, yes — that’s a real front leaf-suspension conversion for the 40-series. BTB currently lists a Series II shackle-reversal kit for 1958–1980 4x-series Land Cruisers including BJ40/FJ40 fitments, and says the current kit has no lift built in; Man-A-Fre also publishes an FJ40 shackle-reversal installation guide.

Whether it’s suggested depends on your setup. BTB’s tech article says shackle reversal is not very effective with flat OEM front springs and, on a stock-spring truck, mainly improves approach angle. The same article says the gains become more meaningful with lifted/arched springs, where ride, handling, and off-road behavior can improve.

It’s also not a tiny bolt-on change. Man-A-Fre’s install guide calls for removing the original hangers, welding new hangers in place, setting hanger position/shackle angle, potentially using caster shims, and checking bump-stop, shock, and front-driveshaft clearance; BTB separately recommends bump-stop offset brackets with its Series II kit.

Front: OME 60097 is 493 mm open / 315 mm closed. The closest current Bilstein J40-length shock is 33-062518, which Bilstein US sells as a B8 5125 at 470.7 mm extended / 306.5 mm collapsed. Bilstein describes the 5125 as a 46 mm monotube sold by length for custom applications, while OME Nitrocharger Sport is twin-tube and tuned around spring/load choices. On a leaf-sprung 40, that usually means Bilstein feels tighter and OME feels calmer/more compliant; that last sentence is my inference from the designs, not a factory quote.

Rear: the two OME paths are very different in length. The shorter rear Sport setup, 60064, is 594 mm open / 360 mm closed. The late-40 rear N76 is 685 mm open / 408 mm closed. So on your 1978, I would not order the rear shocks by year alone: if the lower rear eye is below the leaf spring, follow the N76 path; if it is the shorter/top-of-axle style, follow the 60064-style path.

That same length logic makes the Bilstein side pretty straightforward. 33-185606 is 573.2 / 359 mm, so it is the near-match to the short OME rear. 33-185552 is 658.2 / 404 mm, so it is the near-match to the long N76 rear. Bilstein’s older J4/J5 catalog groups the 40-series under B8 5100 lift applications, but Bilstein US now sells those J40-relevant parts as B8 5125 custom-length shocks. I would not make 5160 the default choice here; the normal J40 route is not a straightforward 5160 catalog application, and Bilstein’s custom reservoir line is 5165.

My pick for your truck: if you want the most “right for a classic 40” answer, I’d lean OME. So: 60097 front, then verify the rear mount on the 1978 before ordering the rear. If it’s the short rear layout, think OME 60064-type or Bilstein 33-185606-type. If it’s the late/long rear layout, think OME N76-type or Bilstein 33-185552-type. If you specifically want a firmer, more controlled feel and don’t mind choosing by actual shock length, Bilstein is excellent — but on a modified FJ40 like yours, I’d choose by measured length and mount style, not by brand first.
Shackle reversal also an option https://parts.resurrectionlandcruisers.com/products/prlc-fj40-shackle-reversal-kit

Assuming you mean the Toyota Land Cruiser J40 / BJ-FJ 40 Series: none of Bilstein’s direct-fit, in-cabin adjustable (“cockpit control”) systems are cataloged for it. Bilstein’s current FAQ says retrofit active setups are their B16 ridecontrol/iRC for specific vehicles, and their DampTronic systems are for vehicles that already have factory electronic damping with cockpit drive-mode control. The current Bilstein Australia BJ/FJ-40 page only shows passive B6 shocks.

What I could verify for a J40:

Current official BJ/FJ-40 listing: B6 standard-height Front B46 0248 and Rear B46 0258.
Older official Bilstein classic catalog data for Land Cruiser J4/J5: passive B6 4600 24-002486 / 24-002585, plus passive B8 5100 lift combos 33-062518 + 33-185606 for about 0–50 mm lift, or 33-185552 + 33-185606 for about 100 mm lift.
Current aftermarket listings still show B6 4600 stock-height sets for 1975–1982 Land Cruiser 40 4WD, and lift listings with B8 5125 for FJ40s, with some sellers splitting pre-1974 vs 1974-up fitment.

So the answer is: no bolt-on Bilstein with cockpit control for a J40. The Bilstein choices I could verify are passive B6 at stock height and passive B8 lift shocks, depending on year and lift.

Yes — bolt-on kits do exist for converting a Land Cruiser J40 to hydraulic power steering, but there’s an important catch:

👉 Almost none are truly 100% bolt-on.

They are usually “conversion kits” that reduce fabrication, but most still require some welding or drilling.

Example hydraulic conversion kits for the J40
Complete Saginaw power steering kit

A typical example is a Saginaw conversion kit like the one from Cruiser Solutions.

It normally includes:

power steering gearbox

steering shaft

pump

pump bracket

mounting plate

pitman arm

drag link

high and low pressure hoses

pulley and belt system

These kits run roughly $1,100–$1,600 depending on completeness.

Installation reality

Even with a kit:

Typical work required:

Weld steering box mounting plate to frame

Drill or cut hole in front crossmember

Modify steering column

Install pump bracket and pulley

The steering box mount must be welded because it carries very high steering forces, and installers recommend a certified welder.

So the kits are “semi-bolt-on”, not truly plug-and-play.

Types of kits you’ll see

There are basically three J40 conversion styles.

1️⃣ Saginaw steering conversion

Most common worldwide.

Uses:

GM steering box

GM pump

Pros:

very strong

easy parts availability

Cons:

most fabrication

2️⃣ Toyota mini-truck box conversion

Uses steering box from:

1979–1985 Toyota pickup / Hilux

Pros:

more compact

more “Toyota”

Cons:

still some welding

3️⃣ FJ60 steering box conversion

Uses steering from the 60-series Land Cruiser.

Pros:

extremely robust

good steering feel

popular with big tyres

Cons:

slightly more complex install

The closest thing to “bolt-on”

There are kits that provide all mounting plates and shafts, so you just install the steering box and pump.

But even those still require:

welding one mount

drilling a crossmember

There’s no true IKEA-style bolt-on hydraulic kit because the original J40 chassis was never designed for power steering.

The real easiest path (most owners do this)

Instead of buying a full kit:

Most Land Cruiser builders simply install:

**Toyota Hilux steering box

Saginaw pump

custom mount plate**

This is cheaper and extremely reliable.

✅ Summary

Option	Difficulty
True bolt-on hydraulic kit	❌ doesn’t really exist
Conversion kit	⚠️ some welding required
Custom Hilux/FJ60 swap	⚠️ moderate fabrication
Electric power steering	✅ easiest install

💡 If you want, I can also show you:

The easiest J40 power steering conversion ever done (very clever design)

The Hilux parts list that makes the conversion extremely cheap

The steering upgrade that makes a J40 drive like a modern 4×4

Those three things are what most serious Land Cruiser builders end up doing.

Converting a J40 to hydraulic power steering is not something you can realistically do from home with no tools. It’s a mechanical fabrication job involving cutting, drilling, and installing major steering components. The safest approach is either buying a full bolt-on kit or having a workshop do the fabrication.

I’ll explain what the minimum realistic setup looks like.

What a hydraulic conversion actually requires

A J40 was designed with a manual steering box and bell-crank system, so power steering requires installing a completely different steering box and linkage.

Core parts required:

Steering components

Power steering steering box (often from Land Cruiser 60/70 or Toyota Hilux)

Pitman arm

Drag link

Modified steering shaft with U-joints

Hydraulic system

Power steering pump

Pump mounting bracket

Reservoir

High-pressure hose

Return hose

Engine drive

Double crank pulley

Drive belt

Frame mounting

Steering box mounting plate

Reinforcement plate

Tools normally required

At minimum:

Basic tools

socket set

spanners

jack + axle stands

breaker bar

Fabrication tools

drill

angle grinder

welder (almost always required)

Without these, the conversion is essentially impossible.

Realistic options if you have no tools
Option 1 — Buy a full conversion kit

Some kits are designed to minimize fabrication.

Example suppliers:

Cruiser Corps

Man-A-Fre

BTB Products

Cool Cruisers

These typically include:

steering box

mounting plate

pump

hoses

steering shaft

Even with a kit you still need:

drilling

some cutting

A garage can install it in 5–8 hours.

Option 2 — Have a 4x4 workshop do it

Best option if starting from nothing.

Typical cost depending on parts:

Parts: $600–1500

Labour: $300–600

In Pakistan the labour is usually much cheaper.

Option 3 — Electric power steering column

If you truly want minimal tools, EPS is the only realistic DIY approach.

Steps are basically:

Remove steering column

Install EPS column motor unit

Wire to battery

Connect to steering box

But even this usually requires:

cutting the steering shaft

welding or adapters

If you want the easiest possible path

The simplest solution for a J40 owner without tools is:

Take it to a Land Cruiser specialist and install a Toyota 60-series power steering box conversion.

This is the most common J40 upgrade worldwide.

💡 If you want, I can also show you:

The easiest J40 power steering conversion ever designed (almost bolt-in)

The cheapest conversion using Toyota Hilux parts

The mistake that cracks the J40 frame during conversion

Those three things save most people a lot of money and frustration.

Originally Answered: Is it possible to clone an auto-feminized plant like any type of other cannabis seeds?
Yes.

It is indeed possible to clone a autoflower. but nonsensical. Auto Flower does not relay on the hours of light if receives. they run a date stamp ,cuts or clones would unfortunately flower at the exact time the mother does. thus you would be left with plus minus the same amount of flower you could have had from a single plant. you could basically say that your just splitting the plant into pieces

Cuts made from regular (photoperiod) plants are able to change between veg and bloom on the amount in hours of light that it receives. allowing for them to be changed from flower to veg and back to flower

Take your cuttings from a plant in the vegetative stage. The vegetative stage begins when the plant is 3-4 weeks old, and the stage lasts for up to 16 weeks before the plant starts blooming. While you can technically take a cutting from any plant, plants in the flowering stage take weeks or even months longer to clone![4]
Ideally, pick a mother plant that’s at least 5 weeks old from seedling and has multiple side shoots.[5]
If you pick a plant that’s too young, the cutting will have hollow inner walls, and it’ll quickly wilt.
